metadata.title: Why You Can Spot a Fake Job Without Reading It
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Reallocation Engine candidate 03. Source chapter: books/the-reallocation-engine/chapters/08-is-the-job-real-ats-detection-and-liveness.md
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, dusty blue = live/real, terracotta = ghost/fake, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: dusty blue #5B7B9C = live / real; terracotta #D35F43 = ghost / fake; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: HOOK CHANGED AT FACTCHECK: the candidate card's 'up to 42%' prevalence did not verify (best current sources: Greenhouse 18-22%, cross-platform 18-27%, ResumeUp.AI 27.4% LinkedIn). Film leads with the VERIFIED stat: 81% of recruiters admit their employer posts ghost jobs (MyPerfectResume survey, n=753). Card exclusions: no ATS taxonomy, THREE fingerprints max on screen, no study litany, no CLI, ONE closing line on what the filter misses.

beats.narration_text: Eighty-one percent of recruiters admit it: their employer posts jobs it isn't hiring for. And the posting's words are the one place you can't tell.

card.copy: Why you can spot a fake job without reading it

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] Why you can spot a fake job without reading it

beats.narration_text: A ghost posting isn't a job that fell through. It was never, at the moment of posting, connected to an intention to hire. A picture of a door.
beats.scene_description: a pristine careers-page job posting printed and pinned to the newsprint ground — crisp headline, tidy bullet points, apply button — slow push-in on the apply button
beats.image_prompt: printed screenshot of a polished corporate job posting page with a prominent apply button, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, desaturated flat print reproduction, editorial collage

beats.narration_text: From the outside it reads exactly like a real one. The description is marketing — and marketing always reads alive.
beats.scene_description: two identical clippings of the posting pinned side by side; the annotation plane drops a charcoal serif label between them: 'one of these is real' — no ring, no X, just the label
beats.image_prompt: two identical printed job-posting clippings pinned side by side on aged newsprint, desaturated flat print, editorial collage

beats.narration_text: But a machine you already trust solves this exact problem a billion times a day, without reading a word: your spam filter.

document.quote: Sent at 3 a.m. Ten thousand copies. No replies.
document.attribution: — what a spam filter actually reads

beats.scene_description: the spam filter's evidence set as a large serif quote card; gold highlighter sweeps 'reads' in the attribution as it is spoken
beats.new_visual_element: [QUOTE] what a spam filter actually reads

beats.narration_text: It doesn't judge what the mail says. It scores how the mail behaves — and weak signals pile into a confident call.

production_viz.label: score the behavior, not the words
production_viz.mechanic: an envelope plate beside a vertical liveness meter; three small terracotta ticks land (3 a.m. blast · no replies · identical copies) and the meter fill drops step by step into the spam zone; a terracotta SPAM chip lands at the bottom

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the spam meter drops in three steps

beats.narration_text: Ghost postings behave, too. A stale clock: up eleven weeks, never once updated.

production_viz.label: fingerprint 1 of 3
production_viz.mechanic: the posting card appears beside its liveness meter (fill high, blue); terracotta stamp 'STALE CLOCK' slaps onto the card at a slight angle; meter fill drops one step
production_viz.note: fingerprint 1 of 3 (card exclusion caps at three)

beats.narration_text: Frozen siblings: ten other roles at the same company, untouched for exactly as long.
beats.scene_description: same chart persists; a row of small frozen sibling-listing cards fades in beneath the posting; terracotta stamp 'FROZEN SIBLINGS' lands; meter drops another step

production_viz.label: fingerprint 2 of 3
production_viz.mechanic: sibling mini-cards appear greyed beneath the posting card; stamp 2 lands; meter fill drops and turns terracotta as it crosses the midline

beats.new_visual_element: [STAMP 2/3] FROZEN SIBLINGS — meter crosses the midline

beats.narration_text: Cloned text: the same description, word for word, as a listing from eighteen months ago.

production_viz.label: fingerprint 3 of 3
production_viz.mechanic: a faded duplicate posting card slides half-out from behind the original like carbon paper; stamp 'CLONED TEXT' lands; meter fill drops deep into the ghost zone
production_viz.note: fingerprint 3 of 3 — no further signals (card exclusion)

beats.new_visual_element: [STAMP 3/3] CLONED TEXT — meter deep in ghost zone

beats.narration_text: No single stamp is decisive — real roles do sit stale at slow companies. But the stamps accumulate, the way no one word makes an email spam.
beats.scene_description: the stamped posting and its low meter persist; the editor's pen draws one terracotta ring around the METER — the accumulation, not any single stamp — timed to 'accumulate'

production_viz.mechanic: end-state of B08 rebuilt; HandRing lands around the meter on 'accumulate'

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] ring around the meter — the pattern, not the word

beats.narration_text: So the same company, the same title, can score opposite ways. One is a door. The other is a picture of a door.

production_viz.label: same employer, two doors
production_viz.mechanic: two posting cards side by side, same title chip; left meter fills high blue and takes a blue LIVE chip; right meter drains low terracotta and takes a terracotta GHOST chip
production_viz.note: the chapter's Posting A / Posting B example, drawn without the five-signal table (card exclusion)

beats.narration_text: The score is a probability, not a verdict. Sometimes a slow company just never updates the page. But it spends your days on doors that actually open.

card.copy: A posting is a signal — signals can lie.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — a posting is a signal
